infobox.value: ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Hercule François*; 18 March 1555[[1]](#cite_note-Birth_date-1) – 10 June 1584) was the youngest son of King [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France") and [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici").

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(July 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French prince (1555–1584)

| Francis | |
| --- | --- |
| *[Duke of Alençon](/wiki/Duke_of_Alen%C3%A7on "Duke of Alençon"), Château-Thierry, [Anjou](/wiki/Duke_of_Anjou "Duke of Anjou"), [Berry](/wiki/Duke_of_Berry "Duke of Berry"), and [Touraine](/wiki/Duke_of_Touraine "Duke of Touraine")* | |
| The Duke in 1572 | |
| Born | 18 March 1555 |
| Died | 10 June 1584(1584-06-10) (aged 29) |
| Burial | 27 June 1584  [Basilica of St Denis](/wiki/Basilica_of_St_Denis "Basilica of St Denis"), France |
| | Names | | | --- | --- | | Hercule François de France | | | |
| [House](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty") | [Valois-Angoulême](/wiki/House_of_Valois-Angoul%C3%AAme "House of Valois-Angoulême") |
| Father | [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France") |
| Mother | [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici") |

*[Monsieur](/wiki/Monsieur "Monsieur")* **François, Duke of Anjou and Alençon** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Hercule François*; 18 March 1555[[1]](#cite_note-Birth_date-1) – 10 June 1584) was the youngest son of King [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France") and [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici").

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

He was scarred by smallpox at age eight, and his pitted face and slightly deformed spine did not suit his birth name of *Hercule*. He changed his name to Francis in honour of his late brother [Francis II of France](/wiki/Francis_II_of_France "Francis II of France") when he was [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation_(Christian_sacrament) "Confirmation (Christian sacrament)").

The royal children were raised under the supervision of the governor and governess of the royal children, [Claude d'Urfé](/wiki/Claude_d%27Urf%C3%A9 "Claude d'Urfé") and [Françoise d'Humières](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7oise_d%27Humi%C3%A8res "Françoise d'Humières"), under the orders of [Diane de Poitiers](/wiki/Diane_de_Poitiers "Diane de Poitiers").

In 1574, following the death of his brother [Charles IX of France](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_France "Charles IX of France") and the accession of his other brother, [Henry III of France](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France"), he became heir to the throne. In 1576 he was made [Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Duke_of_Anjou "Duke of Anjou"), [Touraine](/wiki/Duke_of_Touraine "Duke of Touraine"), and [Berry](/wiki/Duke_of_Berry "Duke of Berry"). His brother Henry III had previously been Duke of Anjou.

## Alençon and the Huguenots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Alençon and the Huguenots")]

During the night of 13 September 1575, Alençon fled from the French court after being alienated from his brother King Henry III as they had had some differences.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Both Henry III and Catherine de' Medici feared he would join the [Protestant rebels](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"). These fears proved well-founded; Francis joined the [prince of Condé](/wiki/Henri,_Prince_of_Cond%C3%A9_(1552%E2%80%931588) "Henri, Prince of Condé (1552–1588)") and his forces in the south. In February 1576, [Henry, King of Navarre](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") escaped from the French court, whereupon his forces also joined Condé. This combined army was enough to force Henry III, without a pitched battle of any sort, to capitulate and sign the very pro-Protestant "peace of Monsieur", or [Edict of Beaulieu](/wiki/Edict_of_Beaulieu "Edict of Beaulieu"), on 6 May 1576. By "secret treaties" which formed part of this peace settlement, many on the Protestant side were rewarded with land and titles. Francis was awarded the [Duchy of Anjou](/wiki/Duchy_of_Anjou "Duchy of Anjou") (along with other lands) and thus became the Duke of Anjou. He had the writer [Jean de La Gessée](/wiki/Jean_de_La_Gess%C3%A9e "Jean de La Gessée") as a secretary.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Courting Elizabeth I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Courting Elizabeth I")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou "Special:EditPage/Francis, Duke of Anjou") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Francis, Duke of Anjou"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Francis%2C+Duke+of+Anjou%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(July 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In 1579, negotiations commenced for marrying Anjou to [Elizabeth I of England](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England"). Anjou sent Jean de Simier, his Master of Wardrobe, to England with a casket of jewels for the Queen. He was a charming courtier and Elizabeth nicknamed him her "singe" or monkey. Anjou wrote to Elizabeth that he would keep her picture by him "till the end of his bones".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Anjou was in fact the only one of Elizabeth's foreign suitors to court her in person. He made two visits to England, in 1579 and 1581.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He was 24 and Elizabeth was 46. Despite the age gap, the two soon became very close, Elizabeth dubbing him her "frog."[[6]](#cite_note-6) While a few believe this nickname arose from a frog-shaped earring he had given her, "frog" has been an unflattering slang nickname for the French for centuries. Whether or not Elizabeth truly planned to marry Anjou is a hotly debated topic.[[7]](#cite_note-7) She was quite fond of him, knowing that he was probably going to be her last suitor.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The match was controversial among the English public: English Protestants warned the Queen that the "hearts [of the English people] will be galled when they shall see you take to husband a Frenchman, and a Papist ... the very common people well know this: that he is the son of the [Jezebel](/wiki/Jezebel "Jezebel") of our age,"[[9]](#cite_note-9) referring to the Duke's mother, [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici"). Among members of Elizabeth's [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council"), only [William Cecil, Lord Burghley](/wiki/Lord_Burghley "Lord Burghley"), and [Thomas Radclyffe, 3rd Earl of Sussex](/wiki/Thomas_Radclyffe,_3rd_Earl_of_Sussex "Thomas Radclyffe, 3rd Earl of Sussex"), supported the marriage scheme wholeheartedly. Most notable councillors, foremost among them [Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester](/wiki/Robert_Dudley,_1st_Earl_of_Leicester "Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester"), and [Sir Francis Walsingham](/wiki/Sir_Francis_Walsingham "Sir Francis Walsingham"), strongly opposed it, even warning the Queen of the hazards of childbirth at her age.

Between 1578 and 1581 the Queen resurrected attempts to negotiate a marriage with the Duke of Alençon. The Duke put himself forward as a protector of the Huguenots and a potential leader of the Dutch.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In these years, Walsingham became friends with the diplomat of [Henry of Navarre](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") in England, the anti-monarchist [Philippe de Mornay](/wiki/Philippe_de_Mornay "Philippe de Mornay").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Walsingham was sent to France in mid-1581 to discuss an Anglo-French alliance, but the French wanted the marriage agreed to first, and Walsingham had instructions to obtain a treaty before committing to the marriage.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He returned to England without an agreement. Personally Walsingham opposed the marriage, perhaps to the point of encouraging public opposition.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Alençon was a Catholic and as his elder brother, Henry III, was childless, he was heir presumptive to the French throne. Elizabeth was probably past the age of childbearing and had no clear successor. If she died while married to the French heir her realms could fall under French control. By comparing the match of Elizabeth and Alençon with the match of the Protestant Henry of Navarre and the Catholic Margaret of Valois, which occurred in the week before the [St Bartholomew's Day massacre](/wiki/St_Bartholomew%27s_Day_massacre "St Bartholomew's Day massacre"), the "most horrible spectacle" he had ever witnessed,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Walsingham raised the spectre of religious riots in England in the event of the marriage proceeding. According to the Earl of Leicester, Elizabeth tolerated his blunt opinions and often unwelcome advice, referring to Walsingham as "her Moor who cannot change his colour" with regard to his strong beliefs.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Eventually Elizabeth pragmatically judged the union an unwise one, after considering the overwhelming opposition of her advisers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] She continued, however, to play the engagement game, if only to warn [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), another of her suitors, of what she might do if it became necessary. Finally Elizabeth bade him farewell in 1581. On his departure she penned a poem, "[On Monsieur's Departure](/wiki/On_Monsieur%27s_Departure "On Monsieur's Departure")", which, taken at face value, has lent credence to the notion that she may really have been prepared to go through with the match.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Anjou in the Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Anjou in the Netherlands")]

Anjou continued on to the Netherlands. In 1579 [William the Silent](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") had invited him to become hereditary sovereign of the [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), and on 29 September 1580 the Dutch [States General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") (with the exception of Zeeland and Holland) had signed the [Treaty of Plessis-les-Tours](/wiki/Treaty_of_Plessis-les-Tours "Treaty of Plessis-les-Tours") with the Duke, who would assume the title "Protector of the Liberty of the Netherlands" and become the sovereign. He did not arrive until 10 February 1582, when William officially welcomed him in [Flushing](/wiki/Flushing,_Netherlands "Flushing, Netherlands"). In spite of the [Joyous Entries](/wiki/Joyous_Entry "Joyous Entry") he was accorded in [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") and his ceremonious installation as [Duke of Brabant](/wiki/Duke_of_Brabant "Duke of Brabant") and [Count of Flanders](/wiki/Count_of_Flanders "Count of Flanders"), Anjou was not popular with the Dutch and Flemish, who continued to see the Catholic French as enemies; the provinces of Zeeland and Holland refused to recognise him as their sovereign, and William, the central figure of the *"[Politiques](/wiki/Politique "Politique")"* who worked to defuse religious hostilities, came under extensive criticism for his "French politics".

* [![The Duke enters Antwerp, greeted by cannons](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Fran%C3%A7ois%2C_Duc_dAnjou%2C_entering_Antwerp_%281582%29_Canon_fire_welcoming_Fran%C3%A7ois%2C_Duc_d%E2%80%99Anjou%2C_and_his_troops_to_the_city_of_Antwerp.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois%2C_Duc_dAnjou%2C_entering_Antwerp_%281582%29_Canon_fire_welcoming_Fran%C3%A7ois%2C_Duc_d%E2%80%99Anjou%2C_and_his_troops_to_the_city_of_Antwerp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois,_Duc_dAnjou,_entering_Antwerp_(1582)_Canon_fire_welcoming_Fran%C3%A7ois,_Duc_d%E2%80%99Anjou,_and_his_troops_to_the_city_of_Antwerp.jpg "The Duke enters Antwerp, greeted by cannons")

  *Satirical Flemish painting painted c.1586, three years after [Anjou's Antwerp fiasco](/wiki/French_Fury "French Fury"); depicting a cow which represents the Dutch provinces. King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") vainly tries to ride the cow, drawing blood with his spurs. Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") feeds it while William of Orange holds it steady by the horns. The cow is defecating on the Duke of Anjou, who holds its tail. ([Toronto Public Library](/wiki/Toronto_Public_Library "Toronto Public Library"))*

He is now thought to have been the patron behind the "[Valois tapestries](/wiki/Valois_tapestries "Valois tapestries")" presented to Catherine de' Medici, which depicted major figures in Catherine's court against scenes of festivity.[[11]](#cite_note-11) When Anjou's French troops arrived in late 1582, William's plan seemed to pay off, as even the [Duke of Parma](/wiki/Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma") feared that the Dutch would now gain the upper hand.

However, Anjou himself, dissatisfied with his limited power, decided to take control of the Flemish cities of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk"), and [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend") by force.

He would personally lead the attack on Antwerp. To fool the citizens of Antwerp, Anjou proposed that he should make a "Joyous Entry" into the city, a grand ceremony in which he would be accompanied by his French troops.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] On 18 January 1583, Anjou entered Antwerp, but the citizens had not been deceived. The city militia ambushed and destroyed Anjou's force in the [French Fury](/wiki/French_Fury "French Fury"). Anjou barely escaped with his life.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Blason_duche_fr_Anjou_%28moderne%29.svg/250px-Blason_duche_fr_Anjou_%28moderne%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Blason_duche_fr_Anjou_(moderne).svg)

Coat-of-arms (after 1576)

The debacle at Antwerp marked the end of Anjou's military career. His mother, [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici"), is said to have written to him that "would to God you had died young. You would then not have been the cause of the death of so many brave gentlemen."[[13]](#cite_note-13) Another insult followed when [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") formally ended her engagement to him after the massacre. The position of Anjou after this attack became impossible to hold, and he eventually left the country in June. His departure also discredited William, who nevertheless maintained his support for Anjou.

Soon Anjou fell seriously ill with "tertian ague", [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuestier2019153-14) Catherine de' Medici brought him back to Paris, where he reconciled with his brother, King [Henry III of France](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France"), in February 1584. Henry even embraced his brother, whom he had famously called *le petit magot* ("little macaque"). By June, the Duke of Anjou was dead.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuestier2019153-14) He was 29. His premature death meant that the [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") [Henry of Navarre](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") became heir-presumptive, thus leading to an escalation in the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion").

## Titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Titles")]

* 1560–1584: Duke of Évreux
* 1566–1584: [Duke of Alençon](/wiki/Duke_of_Alen%C3%A7on "Duke of Alençon"); Duke of Château-Thierry; [Count of Perche](/wiki/Count_of_Perche "Count of Perche"); [Count of Meulan](/wiki/Count_of_Meulan "Count of Meulan"); Count of Mantes
* 1576–1584: [Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Duke_of_Anjou "Duke of Anjou"); [Duke of Berry](/wiki/Duke_of_Berry "Duke of Berry"); [Duke of Touraine](/wiki/Duke_of_Touraine "Duke of Touraine")
* 1580–1584: [Lord of the Netherlands](/wiki/Lord_of_the_Netherlands "Lord of the Netherlands")
* 1582–1584: [Duke of Brabant](/wiki/Duke_of_Brabant "Duke of Brabant"), [Count of Flanders](/wiki/Count_of_Flanders "Count of Flanders")
